step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> MOUSE(row=19, col=48)

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, MOUSE(row=19, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 61, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking targets toggles object positions (reversible). The green player moved from (40,10) to (38,8) due to arrow key presses. The left panel layout is back to near-initial state. Need to discover the actual goal - perhaps positioning the green player near objects matters.
- Plan: Try clicking on the white divider blocks or the yellow square to discover new mechanics. Also try moving the green player to different positions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 60 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I've used 60 actions and still haven't completed the level. I'm over the target budget. Let me look at the current state more carefully.
Looking at the image, I can see:
- The red bar is now horizontal at (30,12)-(33,17)
- The dark red bar is at (30,18)-(33,21)
- The blue block is at (34,8)-(37,11)
- The checkerboard dots are at (20,18)-(23,21)
- The green player is at (38,12)-(39,13)
Wait, the red bar is horizontal now! Looking at the image, I can see:
- Top: checkerboard dots (blue pattern) next to yellow square
- Middle: red bar (horizontal) next to dark red bar
- Bottom: blue block
- Bottom: green player
The red bar is horizontal at (30,12)-(33,17), and the dark red bar is at (30,18)-(33,21). Together they form a horizontal bar at rows 30-33, cols 12-21.
The blue block is at (34,8)-(37,11), and the checkerboard dots are at (20,18)-(23,21).
The green player is at (38,12)-(39,13).
Looking at the right panel:
- Red inverted T: rows 17-21, cols 42-54
- Blue T: rows 34-38, cols 42-54
Hmm, the red bar is horizontal at rows 30-33, which is below the red inverted T's rows 17-21. The blue block is at rows 34-37, which matches the blue T's rows 34-38.
Wait, maybe I need to look at this differently. The red inverted T has:
- Horizontal bar: rows 17-19, cols 44-52
- Vertical stem: rows 19-21, cols 48-49
The blue T has:
- Horizontal bar: rows 36-38, cols 44-52
- Vertical stem: rows 34-36, cols 48-49
On the left panel:
- Red bar (horizontal): rows 30-33, cols 12-17
- Dark red bar: rows 30-33, cols 18-21
- Blue block: rows 34-37, cols 8-11
- Checkerboard dots: rows 20-23, cols 18-21
The red bar and dark red bar form a horizontal bar at rows 30-33, cols 12-21. This is 4 rows x 10 cols.
The blue block is at rows 34-37, cols 8-11. This is 4 rows x 4 cols.
The checkerboard dots are at rows 20-23, cols 18-21. This is 4 rows x 4 cols.
Hmm, the red bar + dark red bar form a horizontal bar, which matches the "horizontal" part of the red inverted T. But the red inverted T also has a vertical stem.
Wait, maybe the game is about "matching" the left panel configuration to the right panel targets. The right panel has two T-shapes, and the left panel has rectangles that need to be arranged to form T-shapes.
But the rectangles can't form T-shapes because they're all rectangles. Unless... the game is about "abstract matching" where the left panel objects need to be arranged in a way that "corresponds" to the right panel targets.
Actually, let me look at the image again. The left panel shows:
- Top: checkerboard dots (blue pattern) next to yellow square
- Middle: red bar (horizontal) next to dark red bar
- Bottom: blue block
- Bottom: green player
The red bar and dark red bar are side by side, forming a horizontal bar. The blue block is below them. The checkerboard dots are above them.
Looking at the right panel:
- Red inverted T: horizontal bar on top, vertical stem on bottom
- Blue T: horizontal bar on bottom, vertical stem on top
Maybe the correspondence is:
- Red inverted T: red bar horizontal (top) + dark red bar as stem (bottom) = but they're side by side, not top/bottom
- Blue T: blue block as stem (top) + checkerboard dots as bar (bottom) = but they're not aligned
This doesn't work. Let me try a different approach.
Actually, I realize I should try clicking on the green player at its current position (38,12). Maybe the green player needs to be at a specific position and then clicked to "submit" the solution.
But I only have a few actions left before the game ends. Let me try clicking on the green player.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on the green player at its current position
# Green player is at (38,12)-(39,13)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 38, 'col': 12}])
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
